macro_segments.title: The Principle of Which Temperature Wins: Rav's 'Ila'ah Gavar' vs. Shmuel's 'Tata'ah Gavar'

micro_segments.title: Introduction to Heat Transfer in Kashrus: Hot into Hot and Cold into Cold
Rav's Position: The Top One Wins Out (Ila'ah Gavar)
Shmuel's Position: The Bottom One Wins Out (Tata'ah Gavar)
Physical Logic Supporting Shmuel: Heat Rises

macro_segments.title: Proof from the Pesach Mishna: Gravy Dripping onto Hot Pottery

micro_segments.title: The Mishna's Case: Pesach Juices Bouncing Back from Hot Pottery
According to Rav: The Pottery Becomes Hot and Re-Roasts the Gravy
Challenge to Shmuel: Why Must You Remove Anything If Everything Cools?
Resolution: The Pottery Was Hot, Not Cold

macro_segments.title: The Flour Case: Gravy Dripping onto Cold Flour

micro_segments.title: Mishna's Second Case: Gravy Dripping into Flour
Rav's Explanation: Hot Gravy Heats Cold Flour, Flour Re-Roasts Gravy
Challenge to Shmuel Again: Cold Flour Should Cool the Gravy
Resolution: We Speak of Boiling Hot Flour, Not Cold

macro_segments.title: Oil Absorbed into Roasted Pesach: Testing the Ila'ah/Tata'ah Debate

micro_segments.title: Mishna: Terumah Oil Rubbed on Pesach—Different Rules for Kohanim and Non-Kohanim
Raw Pesach: Cold on Cold, Just Rinse Off
Roasted Pesach: Must Peel Off Outer Layer
Rav's Explanation: Top Oil is Cold, Bottom Pesach is Hot—Top Wins, Only Peel Outer Layer
Shmuel's Problem: If Bottom Wins, Hot Pesach Should Heat Oil Throughout
Response: Oil is Too Small an Amount to Absorb Deeply—Only Kleipah
Conclusion: Mishna Cannot Prove Either Position

macro_segments.title: Baraita Supporting Shmuel: Tata'ah Gavar as the Ruling

micro_segments.title: Baraita Text: Hot into Hot or Cold into Hot is Forbidden; Hot into Cold or Cold into Cold is Permitted
Gemara's Objection: Hot into Cold Should Require At Least Kleipah
Amended Reading: Hot into Cold Requires Kleipah; Cold into Cold Only Rinse
Second Baraita with Same Logic: Meat and Milk
Gemara's Second Objection: Hot into Cold Meat Should Require Kleipah
Resolution: Amended Reading Again—Hot into Cold Requires Kleipah
Summary: Both Rav and Shmuel Agree on Kleipah When One Thing is Hot

macro_segments.title: The Status of Salted Meat: Maliach K'Rotach

micro_segments.title: Rav Huna: Exception to Rinse Rule—Salted Meat Absorbs Like Hot
Shmuel's Principle: Something Salted is Like Something Boiling
Rava's Limitation: Only Heavily Salted (Inedible from Salt) Counts
Case: Pigeon Salted for Blood Fell into Sour Cream—Rav Chinana Permits Rinsing
Rava Praises Rav Chinana's Reasoning: Pigeon is Edible Despite Salt

macro_segments.title: Additional Exceptions to the Cold Rinse Rule: Roasted, Porous, and Spiced Meat

micro_segments.title: Exception One: Roasted Pesach Even If Cold Requires Kleipah Due to Absorbency
Exception Two: Meat with Cracks and Crevices (Peila) Absorbs Deeply
Exception Three: Heavily Spiced Meat Absorbs Like Salted
General Principle: Cold Meat Cold Liquid Requires Consideration of Absorbency

macro_segments.title: Taste Transfer by Smell (Reicha): Rav vs. Levi on Aroma Absorption

micro_segments.title: Rav's Position: Fatty Kosher Meat Draws Out and Absorbs Taste from Lean Treif Meat Through Air
Levi's Position: Smell Alone (Without Contact) Has No Halachic Effect
Levi's Practical Ruling: Goat and Pig Cooked Together in Same Oven—Goat Kosher

macro_segments.title: Baraita on Two Pesachim Side by Side: Challenge to Both Rav and Levi

micro_segments.title: Baraita: Cannot Cook Two Pesachim Together Due to Mixing
Initial Question: What Mixing—Bodies or Tastes?
Problem for Rav: If Only Taste Transfer, Why Mention Even Goat and Sheep?
Resolution: Two Pots Scenario—Separate Spaces, No Smell Transfer, But Bodies Still Mix
Rav's Position Reinterpreted: Concern is Body-Mixing Not Just Taste

macro_segments.title: Bread and Wine Debate of Tannaim: When Does Reicha Matter?

micro_segments.title: Case: Hot Bread on Barrel of Terumah Wine—Does Aroma Transfer?
Three Tannaic Opinions: Rabbi Meir (Always Forbidden), Rabbi Yehuda (Always Permitted), Rabbi Yossi (Depends on Grain Type)
Gemara's Analysis: Is This a Debate Over Reicha's Halachic Status?
Rav's Response: Everybody Agrees Reicha Matters—Debate is About Circumstances
Three Consensus Cases: Hot Open Barrel (Forbidden), Cold Sealed Barrel (Permitted), Middle Cases (Debated)
Application to Pesachim: Two Pesachim are Like Hot Bread and Open Barrel—All Agree Forbidden

macro_segments.title: Practical Rulings on Reicha: Bread Cooked with Meat and Fish Cooked with Meat

micro_segments.title: Rav Kahana: Bread Cooked with Meat in Oven Cannot Be Eaten with Dairy
Interpretive Question: Does This Prove We Rule Like Rav or Like Levi?
Case of Fish Cooked with Meat: Rava Forbids Eating with Dairy
Mar bar Rav Ashi: Fish and Meat Forbidden Even Alone—Health Concern (Bad Breath, Leprosy)

macro_segments.title: Rambam's Rejection of Health Reasons and Shulchan Aruch's Ruling on Fish and Meat

micro_segments.title: Rambam Principle: Health Advice from Gemara Not Binding Halacha
Shulchan Aruch Ruling: Despite Health Basis, Prohibition Treats as Issur
Practical Extension: From Cooking Together to Eating Cold Fish and Meat Together

macro_segments.title: Practical Applications of Reicha in Modern Ovens: Covering and Ventilation

micro_segments.title: Return to Ila'ah/Tata'ah and Reicha Debates: Unresolved Which Position We Rule
The 'Shtei K'deirot' Exception: Covered Items Can Cook Together Even According to Rav
Practical Application: Wrapping on Airplanes and Purpose of Double Wrapping
Tosafot Extension: Well-Ventilated Ovens May Permit Mixed Cooking Even Without Covering
Rabbeinu Tam Ruling: Based on Rav, Permits Bread Baking with Fleishig in Modern Large Ovens

nataph al solet (gravy drips on flour)
sach ba'shemen shel terumah (oil on Pesach)
ein tzolin shnei pesachim k'echad (cannot roast two Pesachim together)
bread and wine case (hot bread on terumah wine barrel)
Shulchan Aruch (ruling on fish and meat)
